title: Viennese whirls
subtitle: Piped butter biscuits sandwiched with jam and buttercream
excerpt: Soft, buttery biscuits piped in a rosette shape from a very high-fat dough, baked until just set, then sandwiched in pairs with raspberry jam and vanilla buttercream. A British bakery classic.
sourceNotes: Viennese whirls are a British confection loosely inspired by Viennese butter biscuits (Vanillekipferl and related forms). The British version with a jam and buttercream filling became a staple of high-street bakers and afternoon tea from the mid-twentieth century. Mrs Beeton and Eliza Acton document piped butter biscuit forms in their confectionery and pastry chapters; the sandwiched version with buttercream is a British development.

content.text: Viennese whirl dough is very high in butter relative to flour (higher than shortbread) and the dough must be piped rather than rolled because it is too soft to handle. The consistency should hold a piped shape with defined ridges but be soft enough to go through the nozzle without effort. If the dough seems too firm to pipe easily, leave it at room temperature for 5-10 minutes. The biscuits bake at a low temperature to stay tender and pale.

The butter must be genuinely soft (not just at room temperature. It should yield with almost no pressure) and beaten for the full time. Under-beaten butter produces a dough that pipes unevenly and biscuits that spread flat.

Heat the oven to 160°C (140°C fan). Line two baking trays with baking paper. Beat the 250 g {{unsalted-butter}} and 50 g {{icing-sugar}} together until very pale and fluffy, at least 4 minutes. Beat in the {{vanilla-extract}}. Fold in the sifted {{plain-flour}} and {{cornflour}} until combined. Spoon the dough into a piping bag fitted with a large open star nozzle. Pipe rosettes or swirls about 4 cm across onto the lined trays, leaving 3 cm between each. Press the nozzle slightly flat to build the rosette, then release pressure and pull away cleanly.

Bake for 12-14 minutes until the biscuits are set and very pale gold at the edges: they should not colour significantly. Leave on the tray for 5 minutes, then transfer to a rack. Cool completely before filling.

Beat the remaining 100 g {{unsalted-butter}}, 200 g {{icing-sugar}}, and {{vanilla-extract}} until pale and fluffy. Match biscuits in pairs of similar size. Spread a small amount of {{raspberry-jam}} on the flat side of one biscuit and pipe or spoon buttercream on the flat side of the other. Press together gently.
